metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC7: Add an explicit pre-edit proving step (e.g. `git stash && pytest tests/unit/test_harness_provenance.py -k docs -q; git stash pop` — expected: FAILED) so the red-to-green transition is concretely captured, not just asserted in prose.
findings.finding: AC7 asserts that `test_harness_provenance.py::test_docs_document_provenance` must be 'now failing pre-edit, green post-edit', but the only proving command given (`pytest tests/unit/test_harness_provenance.py -k docs -q`) is run post-edit. There is no AC or step that captures a proving command for the pre-edit red state, so an executor could skip confirming the test was actually red before making changes — meaning the green post-edit result proves nothing about the diff (the test might have been passing all along).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sequencing sub-bullet under AC7: 'Pre-condition proof (run before edits): `pytest tests/unit/test_harness_provenance.py -k docs -q` → expect at least one FAILED. Post-condition proof (run after edits): same command → expect all PASSED.' This makes the transition verifiable rather than assumed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit assertions to AC6 (and corresponding test code) that verify the vocabulary form in `docs/event-schema.md` and `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` is also updated — not just `docs/config.md`.
findings.finding: The Scope and What sections name `docs/event-schema.md` and `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` as vocabulary-alignment edit targets, but AC6's proof command (`pytest tests/unit/test_harness_provenance.py -k docs -q`) only verifies the `docs/config.md` change. The existing `test_docs_document_provenance` makes no assertions about the vocab form in `docs/event-schema.md`, and no test at all covers `session_id.py`'s comment text — leaving two of the three named edit targets with no automated proof.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_docs_document_provenance` (or add a sibling test) to assert: `'claude-code_<ver>' not in ev` (event-schema) and `'base name \`claude-code\`'` or equivalent new form is present; and a similar check for the `session_id.py` comment text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the planned additions to `test_docs_document_provenance` (asserting the old `claude-code_<ver>` slash-form is ABSENT and `base name \`claude-code\`` is PRESENT in `docs/config.md`) are actually specified in the plan's AC6 implementation instructions — the current function body lacks these assertions and would pass pre-edit, contradicting the 'now failing pre-edit' claim.
findings.finding: The existing `test_docs_document_provenance` function (lines 191–197 of `tests/unit/test_harness_provenance.py`) currently asserts only `'AI_AGENT'` and `'OPENCODE_SESSION_ID'` in `docs/config.md`, and `'claim_harness'` / `'claim_remote_session'` in `docs/event-schema.md` — it contains NO assertion about the old `claude-code_<ver>` slash-form or the new `'base name \`claude-code\`'` phrase. AC6 claims this test will be 'now failing pre-edit, green post-edit', but as written it would pass both before AND after the vocabulary change, so the proof mechanism does not actually verify the targeted edit.
findings.suggested_fix: Explicitly state in AC6 that the implementer must ADD two new assertions to `test_docs_document_provenance`: `assert 'claude-code_<ver>' not in cfg` (or the specific slash-form) and `assert 'base name \`claude-code\`' in cfg`, so the test is red before the edit and green after.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete updated canonical `AI_AGENT` vocabulary (all valid base names and the optional-suffix rule) in one authoritative location (e.g. the `_HARNESS_VAR` comment in `session_id.py` or a dedicated vocabulary note), and extend `test_resolve_harness` to assert that bare `claude-code` (no version suffix) is a valid accepted value.
findings.finding: The plan introduces bare `claude-code` as a new valid `AI_AGENT`/`claim_harness` enum value and changes `_<version>` from required to optional, but never defines the complete updated canonical vocabulary. All existing codebase artifacts (docs/config.md:352, docs/event-schema.md:145, src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py:45, test_harness_provenance.py:60) enumerate only the versioned form `claude-code_<ver>`. The plan's 'Vocabulary alignment' step gives only prose intent — 'base name with optional `_<version>` suffix' — without specifying the exhaustive value set or a machine-checkable pattern, leaving the executor to decide what other base names (e.g. `codex`, `cursor`, `opencode`) now also shed their `_<ver>` requirement.
findings.location: What section — 'Vocabulary alignment'; AC6; src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py:45; docs/config.md:352; docs/event-schema.md:145
findings.suggested_fix: In `session_id.py` update the `_HARNESS_VAR` comment to read: 'Opaque harness-provenance tag. Accepted forms: base name alone (e.g. `claude-code`, `opencode`, `codex`, `cursor`) OR base name with version suffix (e.g. `claude-code_1.2.3`). The `_<version>` suffix is optional.' Then add one assertion to `test_resolve_harness`: `monkeypatch.setenv("AI_AGENT", "claude-code"); assert resolve_harness() == "claude-code"` — and include `test_resolve_harness` in the AC6 modification list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the unwritable `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` scenario (write error → silent exit 0) to the What section's no-op enumeration so the description and AC3 are fully consistent.
findings.finding: The What section enumerates exactly three no-op cases (parse failure, missing/blank session_id, absent CLAUDE_ENV_FILE) but AC3 adds a fourth — an UNWRITABLE CLAUDE_ENV_FILE (path under a non-existent dir) — that the What section never mentions. An implementer following the description alone might let a write-error propagate as a non-zero exit, directly contradicting what AC3 requires (silent exit 0).
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, extend the no-op sentence to: '…any parse failure, a missing/blank `session_id`, an absent `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`, OR a write error (e.g. path under a non-existent directory) is a silent no-op with exit 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the TOCTOU race on CLAUDE_ENV_FILE: either (a) use `flock` or an atomic write (write to a temp file in the same dir then `mv`), or (b) explicitly state and verify the Claude Code hooks documentation guarantees serial hook execution, so the assumption is grounded and visible to future maintainers.
findings.finding: The strip-then-rewrite of CLAUDE_ENV_FILE (read lines → filter → overwrite) is a classic TOCTOU race: if two hooks execute concurrently and both read the file before either writes, the second writer silently discards the first writer's changes. The plan does not state that Claude Code serialises hook execution, does not use an atomic write (temp-file + rename), and does not use a file lock — leaving concurrent hook invocations able to corrupt each other's env-file contributions.
findings.location: What — Hook script idempotency description; AC5 (idempotent re-fire)
findings.suggested_fix: Use a temp-file + atomic rename pattern in the shell script: write the new content to `${CLAUDE_ENV_FILE}.shim_tmp.$$`, then `mv` it to `$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`. This is atomic on POSIX filesystems for same-directory renames. Alternatively, wrap the read-filter-write block with `flock -x "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"` if `flock` is available. Add a note in docs/session-id-shims.md citing the Claude Code hooks serialisation guarantee (if it exists) or the atomic-write approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'already a rebar dependency' with 'a system prerequisite (like `git`)' and add a `python3` requirement callout to the `docs/session-id-shims.md` prerequisites section — consistent with how `pyproject.toml` frames system deps.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `python3` is 'already a rebar dependency' to justify its use in the hook script, but `pyproject.toml` confirms `python3` is NOT a package dependency (not in `[project.dependencies]`, not in any extra). It is a system prerequisite alongside `git` and `acli` (described as such in pyproject.toml comments). Framing it as 'a rebar dependency' is factually wrong per the codebase and could cause the install docs or `docs/session-id-shims.md` to omit the required `python3` system-prerequisite callout.
findings.location: What section — 'Uses `python3` (already a rebar dependency) to parse the JSON robustly'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'Uses `python3` (already a rebar dependency)' to 'Uses `python3` (a system prerequisite, like `git` — not a pip dep; document as a prerequisite in `docs/session-id-shims.md`)'. Add a 'Prerequisites' section to `docs/session-id-shims.md` listing `python3 >= 3.11` as required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the TOCTOU race in the idempotent re-fire step: the strip-filter-write on `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is non-atomic, and the plan's own G1 subagent scenario produces concurrent hook fires on the same file — specify an atomic mechanism (e.g., `flock` on the env file, or write-to-temp-then-`mv` for atomic replace) so the 'exactly ONE export, latest value wins' invariant holds under concurrent invocations.
findings.finding: The idempotent re-fire mechanism (read CLAUDE_ENV_FILE → strip prior exports → write fresh exports) is a non-atomic read-modify-write, making the 'never accumulates duplicate/stale lines' invariant a hidden atomicity assumption that breaks under concurrent execution. The plan explicitly names the exact scenario that triggers this — subagents and multiple hook fires on resume/clear/compact (G1 caveat) — yet offers no locking or atomic-replace around the strip+rewrite step. Two concurrent SessionStart hooks reading before either writes will each filter the same original content, and the later writer will clobber the earlier one's vars, potentially leaving the env file with only one hook's exports (or in a corrupt intermediate state if writes interleave at the OS level).
findings.location: What section — 'Idempotent: re-firing on resume/clear/compact first STRIPS this hook's own prior REBAR_SESSION_ID/AI_AGENT exports from the env file … then re-writes the current values'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `flock` (available on Linux/macOS) around the read-filter-write block, or write to a per-invocation temp file then `mv` atomically over the target. For example: `flock "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE" python3 -c '...'` or write to `${CLAUDE_ENV_FILE}.tmp.$$` then `mv "${CLAUDE_ENV_FILE}.tmp.$$" "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"` (which is atomic at the filesystem level). Add a test case in `test_session_shims.py` that fires two concurrent invocations and asserts exactly one REBAR_SESSION_ID line in the result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is the correct, current Claude Code CLI env-injection variable name by citing a specific versioned reference (e.g., a pinned docs URL, a Claude Code release note, or the actual `claude` binary help/source), and document the version/date of that confirmation in both the script header and `docs/session-id-shims.md`.
findings.finding: `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` — the env-var name that the entire injection mechanism depends on — appears zero times anywhere in the repo (src/, docs/, tests/, scripts/) and is not independently verifiable from the plan's text; the sole citation is a URL to `code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks` that cannot be tool-checked. If this var name is wrong or has changed since the citation was written, the shim silently no-ops for every user, making the feature dead-on-arrival while appearing deployed. The plan's graceful-degradation argument presupposes the var name is correct; it is not a substitute for resolving the assertion.
findings.location: What section: 'when `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is set, APPENDS `export REBAR_SESSION_ID=<id>`…' and 'per the Claude Code hooks reference (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or script-header comment that pins the verification: e.g., 'Verified against Claude Code v1.x.x docs at <specific URL> on <date>; the env-injection file mechanism is documented at <anchor>'. Also add a `docs/session-id-shims.md` troubleshooting note explicitly stating 'if `echo $REBAR_SESSION_ID` is empty and `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is unset, the Claude Code version you are running may use a different mechanism — check `claude hooks --help` or the current docs at <URL>.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `python3` availability assertion: either (a) document that the hook requires `python3` ≥3.11 on PATH and add a startup check with a clear error message to stderr (still exit 0 to not block the session), or (b) use `jq` (already a documented dev dependency in CONTRIBUTING/Makefile) or a pure-shell `grep`/`sed` fallback for the `session_id` extraction to avoid the `python3`-on-PATH assumption.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `python3` is 'already a rebar dependency' to justify its use for JSON parsing in the hook script, but `pyproject.toml`'s `requires-python = ">=3.11"` governs the installed rebar package's Python interpreter — not the `python3` binary on `$PATH` in a Claude Code hook subprocess. A user may have rebar installed in a venv with no ambient `python3` on PATH, or the PATH `python3` may be an incompatible version. This is a distinct execution context from the rebar package's runtime.
findings.location: What section: 'Uses `python3` (already a rebar dependency) to parse the JSON robustly'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the two load-bearing Claude Code hook contract facts — the exact JSON field name in the SessionStart stdin envelope and the exact env-injection variable name — against the live Claude Code documentation (confirm the correct URL, whether `code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks` or `docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks`) before shipping, and record the verified field/var names as a comment in the script header alongside the confirmed URL.
findings.finding: The two load-bearing hook contract facts — the `session_id` JSON field name in the SessionStart stdin envelope and the `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` env-injection variable name — are cited to `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks`, a URL that cannot be verified from this environment and whose domain (`code.claude.com`) does not match the known canonical Claude Code documentation domain (`docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks`). If either fact is wrong, the shim silently no-ops for all local-CLI users — exactly the failure mode the plan was designed to prevent — while appearing correctly installed until an operator runs the manual verification.
findings.location: What section: 'the Claude Code hook-contract facts — the SessionStart stdin `session_id` field and the `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` env-injection file — per the **Claude Code hooks reference** (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the script header comment, replace the unverified URL with the confirmed canonical URL and note the exact verified field name and env-var name. If the correct URL cannot be confirmed pre-implementation, add a one-line SPIKE task: 'Confirm SessionStart stdin schema field name and env-injection var name against live `claude --help hooks` or official docs before implementing the shim.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a repo-wide audit step (e.g., `grep -r 'claude-code_'`) to confirm no other docs/source files contain the old slash-form after the vocabulary change, or explicitly declare them out-of-scope with a rationale.
findings.finding: The plan updates the `claude-code_<ver>` vocabulary in three named files (docs/config.md, docs/event-schema.md, session_id.py) but does not audit or update any other locations in the repo that may contain the old slash-form example. If the old form appears in a README, CHANGELOG, or other docs file, those references become stale/inconsistent after the change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Pre-merge: run `grep -rn "claude-code_" docs/ README* CHANGELOG*` and update or explicitly exclude any hits.' Alternatively, extend the AC6 assertion to cover the full docs/ directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Emit at least a single stderr/debug line (e.g., `echo '[claude-code-session-start] no-op: <reason>' >&2`) for each no-op branch, so operators can see the reason in Claude Code's hook-execution output or a redirect log without having to run the manual verification procedure.
findings.finding: When the shim silently no-ops (malformed JSON, blank session_id, or absent CLAUDE_ENV_FILE), it emits nothing observable at runtime — no stderr message, no log line. The only observability path is a manual post-hoc procedure documented in the docs page. An operator who has not read the docs (or who forgets) will have no signal at all that the hook fired but produced nothing, and cannot distinguish 'hook not installed' from 'hook installed but silently failed'.
findings.location: What — 'GRACEFUL DEGRADATION' and 'fail-safe OBSERVABLE' paragraphs; AC3; AC4
findings.suggested_fix:
In each early-exit branch of the shell script, add a short stderr message, e.g.:
```sh
echo "[rebar/claude-code-session-start] no-op: CLAUDE_ENV_FILE not set" >&2
```
This is compatible with the fire-and-forget / exit-0 contract (stderr is not a blocking error), adds zero coupling, and makes every failure mode immediately visible in whatever console or log captures hook stderr.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `description` to `claim_harness` in `ticket_state.schema.json` (and `chn` in `ticket_state_llm.schema.json`) that states the valid-value convention — 'opaque harness tag; base name (e.g. `claude-code`) with optional `_<version>` suffix' — so the processing rule is co-located with the schema field.
findings.finding: The 'base name + optional `_<version>` suffix' vocabulary rule for `AI_AGENT` is stated only in the plan's What prose and AC6's doc assertion, but is never co-located with the schema that downstream consumers (`claim_harness` in `ticket_state.schema.json`, the LLM schema `chn` field) rely on. The schema at `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_state.schema.json:24` defines `claim_harness` as a free `[string, null]` with no pattern, and the plan does not propose adding one — so the decision rule (what constitutes a valid harness tag) lives only in human-readable docs, not in the machine-readable schema.
findings.suggested_fix: In ticket_state.schema.json change line 24 to: `"claim_harness": { "type": ["string", "null"], "description": "Opaque harness-provenance tag from AI_AGENT. Form: base-name (e.g. 'claude-code', 'opencode', 'codex', 'cursor') with optional '_<version>' suffix. Read verbatim." }`. Mirror the description in ticket_state_llm.schema.json chn field.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
